turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Gorsuch has the opinion of the Court this morning in case number 16-285, Epic Systems Corp. versus Lewis and the consolidated cases.

turns.name: JUSTICE GORSUCH
turns.text: These cases differ in detail but in substance present the same question. If an employer and an employee agree to arbitrate claims between them in individualized proceedings is that agreement enforceable under the Federal Arbitration Act or does the National Labor Relations Act displace the Arbitration Act and guarantee employees' right to class or collective actions no matter what? The relative merits of arbitration and class actions are debatable as a matter of policy, but the law is clear. In the Arbitration Act, Congress has instructed courts to enforce arbitration agreements according to their terms. The NLRA does not conflict with these directions. It secures employees' rights to organize unions and bargain collectively but it says nothing about the kinds of litigation procedures that should govern disputes that leave the workplace and enter the courtroom. Let's start with the Arbitration Act. It provides that arbitration agreements shall be valid, irrevocable and enforceable, and that the arbitration shall proceed in accordance with the terms of the agreement. This Court has long recognized that these words establish a liberal federal policy favoring arbitration requiring courts to rigorously enforce arbitration agreements according to their terms, and so the statute before us would seem pretty clearly to mandate enforcement of the terms here requiring arbitration to proceed on an individualized basis. Seeking to avoid this result, the employees point of the Arbitration Act's savings clause which allows courts to refuse enforcement of arbitration agreements save upon such grounds as exist at law or in equity for the revocation of any contract. The employees say that the NLRA makes class action waivers illegal and so renders their arbitration agreements unenforceable under the saving clause. But even assuming, just for the moment, that the NLRA renders class action waivers illegal, that defense still would not qualify for the saving clause. That's because the clause covers only generally applicable contract defenses, not defenses that single out arbitration for disfavored treatment particularly and because the employees before us argue that their arbitration agreements are enforceable trust because they require bilateral arbitration, their defense doesn't fall within the savings clause. As a result, the Arbitration Act requires enforcement of the agreements before us. Next, the employees argue that even if the Arbitration Act makes the arbitration agreements here enforceable the National Labor Relations Act makes some unenforceable. That kind of an agreement faces a stout uphill climb. When faced with two laws allegedly touching on the same topic this Court is not at liberty to pick and choose among them based on which one might be its favorite, but must instead seek to give effect to all of Congress' work. To that end our precedents require a clear and manifest congressional intention before we will read one statute is conflicting with and displacing another. We don't see anything like that here. The NLRA contains not one word about arbitration or the procedures governing class and collective actions. In reply the employees point us to Section 7 of the NLRA and that provision protects workers' "right to self organization the form, join or assist labor organizations, to bargain collectively and to engage in other concerted activities for the purpose of collective bargaining of other mutual aid or protection." The employees say that last catchall term "other concerted activities for the purpose of other mutual aid or protection" can be read to include class or collective actions. We cannot agree. General catchall terms like this appearing at the end of a list of specific examples are usually understood to encompass only things similar in nature to the preceding examples and so we read other concerted activities to protect things like organizing a collective bargaining that employees do in exercising their right to free association in the workplace. That catchall term does not extend far beyond the rest of the list's examples to dictate procedures that govern disputes that leave the workplace and enter the courtroom. In short, the NLRA does not present a clear and manifest conflict with the Arbitration Act and so cannot displace the Arbitration Act's command to enforce agreements before us. Today's dissent sees things a little differently. In its view the Court's decision ushers us back to the Lochner era when courts regularly overrode legislative policy judgments. The dissent even accuses the Court of resurrecting the long dead yellow-dog contract. Respectfully, this is a false alarm. As dissent recognizes the NLRA protects first and foremost workers' rights to unionize and to engage in collective bargaining. Those rights stand as strong today as they did yesterday, and far from reviving the yellow-dog contract against union organizing the Congress outlawed in 1935. Our decision today merely declines to read into the NLRA a new right to class arbitration that the National Labor Relations Board's own general counsel rejected as recently as 2010. The dissent's real complaint lies not so much with today's decision but with a mountain of settled precedent on which it rests. To avoid the result we reach today the dissent is forced to argue that at least a half a dozen of this Court's precedents are wrong and should be overturned, even though no one in this case has asked us to revisit those precedents. After advancing a cramped construction of the Arbitration Act foreclosed by this Court's precedents the dissent offers the most expensive possible reading of the NLRA and orders suggested covers class actions. In support of that reading the dissent relies on legislative history but the people are governed by the law on the books not by the legislative history hidden behind them. The dissent also argues that recognizing a right to class action procedures under the NLRA will better promote enforcement of wage and hour laws. No doubt class actions can help plaintiffs assert individually small claims by spreading the cost of litigation but there's no reason to expect the NLRA to regulate wage and hour litigation. Instead of statutes like the Fair Labor Standards Act which actually regulates wages and hours, and it turns out that the Fair Labor Standards Act does expressly authorize collective lawsuits. This Court held decades ago that this mechanism still allows parties to contract for individual arbitration as they did here. The more fundamental problem with the dissent's argument though is it ultimately rests on a judgment about wise economic policy and courts are not free to substitute their preferred economic policies for those chosen by the people's representatives. We had always understood that to be Lochner's greatest sin. One last point, the dissent calls for Congressional correction of our decision today and Congress is of course free to amend the statutes we have discussed, but until it does so our job is simply to apply them and as currently written the statutes before us plainly require courts to enforce agreements for individualized arbitration and so that alone is where our duty lies. The judgments in Epic number 16-285 and Ernst & Young, number 16-300, are reversed and the cases are remanded for further proceedings consistent with this opinion. The judgment in Murphy Oil 16-307 is affirmed. Justice Thomas has filed a concurring opinion. Justice Ginsburg has filed a dissenting opinion in which Justices Breyer, Sotomayor, and Kagan have joined.
